ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Aloo Chaat; closest match is potato-based snack food (FOODON_03301710). Exact Aloo Chaat entry not present in FoodOn as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Aloo Chaat is approximately 150 grams, equivalent to one small bowl as commonly served at Indian street food stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for a composite Aloo Chaat preparation comprising fried/boiled potatoes, tamarind chutney, green chutney, and chaat masala. Values represent a typical recipe formulation. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried potato entries (FDC ID 170032) to supplement ICMR-NIN data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Aloo Chaat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 estimated using fried potato (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr) as the primary carbohydrate contributor. Tamarind chutney and chaat masala contribute additional sugars, supporting a high GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 22.8) / 100 ≈ 16.4 for a 150g serving (22.8g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (260 mg per 100g) from chaat masala, tamarind chutney, and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Aloo Chaat due to its high sodium content of approximately 390 mg per 150g serving, which represents a significant proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and moderate carbohydrate content from fried potatoes and sweetened chutneys
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Aloo Chaat due to its high GI of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of ~16.4 per 150g serving, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.6g per 100g) and elevated sodium from deep-frying and spice blends
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Aloo Chaat only occasionally, as the combination of saturated fat from frying oil and high sodium from chaat masala and chutneys may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Chaat is a popular North Indian street food snack made from cubed potatoes that are either boiled or deep-fried until crispy, then tossed with a blend of aromatic spices including chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, red chili powder, and black salt. The dish is finished with sweet tamarind chutney and spicy green coriander-mint chutney, giving it a characteristic tangy, spicy, and slightly sweet flavour profile. The texture contrasts soft or crispy potato with the liquid chutneys. Nutritionally, a 150g serving provides approximately 168 kcal, 4.8g protein, 6g fat, and 22.8g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~390 mg per serving) and has a high glycemic index (~72), making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. It is naturally vegetarian and vegan. Aloo Chaat is widely consumed as an evening snack, street food, or appetizer across North India and is commonly found at Indian street food stalls and restaurants globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Aloo Chaat?
Is Aloo Chaat good for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in Aloo Chaat?
What is the glycemic index of Aloo Chaat?
Can I eat Aloo Chaat if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for a composite Aloo Chaat preparation. No single exact entry for Aloo Chaat exists in ICMR-NIN; values represent a recipe-level estimate based on constituent ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from fried potato data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite and estimated nature of the nutritional data.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Aloo Chaat is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): No significant vitamin D source is present in this plant-based preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0: Confirmed negligible/zero for home-style or street-food preparation using standard vegetable oils without industrial hydrogenation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: Detailed fatty acid breakdown for composite Aloo Chaat preparation not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2024. water_g set to -1: Moisture content for composite Aloo Chaat not reported in ICMR-NIN as of 2024. 3. Cholesterol value of 6.0 mg per 100g is low but plausible given trace amounts from frying oil; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central fried potato data. 4. Dietary tag 'low_sugar' from input retained with caution: sugar content of 4.8g per 100g from chutneys is moderate; this tag may not be appropriate for all dietary frameworks. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on fried potato GI from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact Aloo Chaat entry exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2024.
